retrieved_docs.title: Myoclonic Epilepsy and Ragged Red Fibers -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Mitochondria are important cellular organelles and contain their own deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA). The mtDNA is a small circular molecule composed of 16,569 base-pairs and consists of 37 genes. These genes encode for 22 transfer RNAs (tRNAs), 13 polypeptides, and 2 ribosomal RNAs (rRNAs). These genes are important for the mitochondrial respiratory chain (MRC) to generate cellular energy in the form of adenosine triphosphate (ATP). The mtDNA does not follow the laws of Mendelian inheritance and has a strict maternal inheritance. During fertilization, the zygote receives mitochondria only from the oocyte. Thus, mtDNA is transmitted vertically from mother to all her male and female offsprings. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Melas Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: MELAS is a mitochondrial inherited genetic disorder caused by alterations in mitochondrial DNA. Paternal mitochondria are present only in the tailpiece of sperm, which proves that mitochondrial inheritance is maternal. Maternally inherited mitochondrial disorders, including MELAS, result from the loss of mitochondria during fertilization. In rare instances, MELAS may result from a sporadic variation without a familial history. Mitochondrial genetic disorders stem from sequence variations that impair mitochondrial function, including oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) and energy production.

retrieved_docs.title: Myoclonic Epilepsy and Ragged Red Fibers -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Patient and family education is very important regarding the progressive and multisystem nature of this disease. A male with MERRF having a mutated mtDNA cannot transmit the disease to any of his offspring. A female having a mutated mtDNA will transmit the disease to all of her offspring. Further, prenatal diagnosis for MERRF is also possible if MERRF has been detected in the mother.

retrieved_docs.title: Leber Hereditary Optic Neuropathy (LHON) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: LHON was the first disease to be associated with mtDNA point mutations and is, therefore, maternally inherited. Whereas both the father and the mother contribute to the nuclear portion of the zygote, the mother's ovum is virtually the sole provider of the zygote's cytoplasmic contents, which contains several hundred intracytoplasmic mitochondria. "Maternal inheritance" refers to the transmission of the mitochondrial genome from a mother to all her children, with no paternal mtDNA contribution. The mitochondria generate the cellular energy necessary for normal cellular function and maintenance; thus, those cells in tissues particularly reliant on mitochondrial energy production (eg, the central nervous system, including the optic nerve, retinal pigment epithelium, and extraocular muscles) will contain more mitochondria. If a new mutation occurs in the mtDNA and the proportion of mutant mtDNA exceeds a certain tissue-specific threshold, however, the wildtype mtDNA is no longer able to compensate to sustain normal cellular function, and the disease phenotype is expressed. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Autosomal Recessive -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: In contrast, when a family has affected individuals in every generation, this is considered vertical transmission (the affected individuals trace multiple vertical lines through the pedigree). Vertical transmission is characteristic of an autosomal dominant disease. Additionally, consanguinity in a family affected with an autosomal recessive condition will often lead to children afflicted with the condition. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Melas Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Although both genders are equally susceptible to the condition, only women can transmit MELAS as mitochondria are carried in the tails of sperm cells and are consequently shed outside the zygote during fertilization. No apparent racial predilection exists.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Autosomal Dominant -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: First, the Law of Dominance and Uniformity states that some alleles, which are variants of a particular gene found at the same chromosomal locus or location, are dominant over the other alleles for a given gene. Those traits that are not dominant are termed recessive. If an organism inherits at least one dominant variant, then it will display the effect, or phenotype, of the dominant allele. [1] Second, the Law of Segregation states that the two alleles for each gene separate from each other during gametogenesis so that the parent may only pass off one allele; thus, the offspring can only inherit one allele from each parent. Third, the Law of Independent Assortment (Law of Reassortment) states that the alleles of different genes segregate independently of one another during gametogenesis and are distributed independently of one another in the next generation. [2] This concept was later verified with chromosomes, though also disproven in some instances. For example, when genes occur on the same chromosome, they can be linked and not follow this law.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Autosomal Recessive -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Diseases transmitted in an autosomal recessive pattern present when an individual inherits two recessive disease alleles; thus, the individual will be affected with the disease phenotype. Following Mendel’s Law of Segregation, this means that both parents must have at least one disease allele that they can pass on to their child. The easiest way to determine the inheritance pattern of a disorder in a family is by looking at a pedigree. Autosomal recessive diseases typically affect both females and males equally. Autosomal recessive patterns manifest by skipping generations as the affected are usually children of unaffected carriers.  It is also common to see affected individuals with unaffected offspring. This type of pattern demonstrates horizontal transmission because the affected individuals are often seen in more than one child in a family and not in all generations (the affected individuals trace a horizontal line through the pedigree).

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Autosomal Recessive -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: An exception to the horizontal transmission seen in most cases of autosomal recessive disorders occurs in the case of pseudodominance.  In these cases, there will be affected individuals in more than one generation. [3] Pseudodominance occurs when a carrier parent (Dd) has a child with an affected person (dd).  In this case, there is a 50% chance that the child will be affected, and there is a vertical transmission pattern on a pedigree; this is distinguishable from autosomal dominant traits by tracing the pedigree and looking for autosomal recessive characteristics elsewhere in the pedigree. These characteristics include affected parents who have unaffected children and unaffected parents who have affected children.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Muscle Myocyte -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Mitochondrial myopathies are a group of rare disorders that usually present with myopathy and other manifestations. These disorders have mitochondrial inheritance patterns, which means that only mothers can pass mitochondrial DNA onto their offspring. In other words, an affected female will give mutated mitochondrial DNA to all of her offspring, but an affected male (who got the disease from his mother) will pass the mutated mitochondrial DNA to none of his offspring. These disorders demonstrate heteroplasmy, which is variable expression within a population. Imagine two siblings inherit mutated mitochondrial DNA from their mother. Heteroplasmy means that one of the siblings may be affected while the other is not, or the degree of their disease expression may not be equal. Symptoms of these disorders include skeletal muscle myopathy and myalgia (pain), smooth muscle myopathy (dysphagia), and cardiomyopathy. There are many other symptoms associated with the various syndromes. Diagnostic tests include muscle biopsies which may reveal “ragged red fibers” (accumulation of pathologic mitochondria) and genetic testing. No definitive treatment has demonstrated significant efficacy in treating these disorders. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, X-Linked Inheritance -- Introduction -- X linked Recessive Inheritance
retrieved_docs.content: RISK CALCULATION: The X chromosome from a male is transmitted to daughters, and the Y chromosome is transferred to sons. If an affected male has kids with a healthy female, none of his male offsprings will be affected, but all of his female offspring will be carriers.  If a carrier female has kids with a healthy male, each male offspring has a 50% chance of being affected, and female offspring have a 50% chance of being a carrier.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Autosomal Recessive -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Mendelian genetics focuses on disorders caused by single genes, thus excluding polygenic or multifactorial traits. Mendelian genetics has its basis on the experiments of Gregor Mendel, the Austrian monk who worked on garden peas. Mendel described three laws. [1] The first law is the Law of Segregation, which states that each gamete contains one allele for a trait, and it combines with the other gamete to give the offspring a pair of alleles for each trait, one from each parent. The second law is the Law of Independent Assortment, which states that alleles for different traits may segregate and combine independently of each other during meiosis and fertilization. The third law is the Law of Dominance, which states that one allele can be dominant to the other, thereby masking the other if both are present. The masked allele, in this case, is known as the recessive allele.

retrieved_docs.title: Char Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic counseling plays a significant role in the management of Char syndrome, providing families with information about the inheritance pattern of the condition and the potential risk of having affected offspring in future pregnancies. [1] Char syndrome follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern. [21] In individuals with Char syndrome who have an affected parent, there is a 50% chance of passing the mutated gene on to their children. However, Char syndrome can also occur in those with no family history of the condition due to a new mutation in the TFAP2B gene. These are known as de novo mutations, and the risk to the siblings of a proband appears to be low. Prenatal diagnosis can be performed through chorionic villus sampling (CVS) or amniocentesis to identify the presence of the mutation in the developing fetus. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Autosomal Recessive -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Inheritance patterns illustrate disease transmission amongst generations of a family. An autosomal pattern of inheritance occurs in families affected with a genetic disease whose gene is not on a sex chromosome.  Patients affected with autosomal recessive (AR) diseases have a disease allele on each chromosome. The pattern of individuals affected with an AR disease can be traced through a family to determine which individuals are carriers and which individuals are likely to become impacted.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Autosomal Dominant -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: If one parent is heterozygous (i.e., Aa) and affected with the disorder, approximately 50% of the offspring have the chance of inheriting a dominant allele and thus being affected with the disorder, as previously mentioned. If both parents are heterozygous and affected by the disorder, 75% of the offspring have the chance of inheriting a dominant allele and being affected by the disorder. If one parent is homozygous (i.e., AA) and affected by the disorder, all of the offspring may have the chance of inheriting a dominant allele and being affected by the disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Myotonic Dystrophy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Full penetrance alleles of more than 50 are associated with disease manifestations. In CMD, repeats are usually more than 1000, compared to less than 37 in normal individuals, and 38-49 in premutation allele patients (asymptomatic). Offspring of premutation patients can inherit longer repeats, increasing the risk of disease and decreasing the age of onset in the next generations (anticipation). Contrary to the classic (adult), the maternal transmission is up to ~90% of cases, and only 9-12% are paternal. The mechanism is not yet well understood; however, maternal and intrauterine environment are considered contributing factors. [3] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: and cardiovascular complications are a subset of the overall perinatal condition spectrum. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2329
retrieved_docs.content: Infancy is identified as a specific developmental stage that belongs to the broader category of the period of life between birth and death, situating this early phase within the overall lifespan continuum.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2320
retrieved_docs.content: The gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is directly linked to the outcome of a liveborn infant, indicating that reaching this stage of pregnancy can result in a newborn who is alive at birth. In the broader classification of pregnancy timelines, the concept of Duration of gestation encompasses liveborn as a specific category or outcome, reflecting that livebirth is considered one of the possible results within the overall spectrum of gestational durations. This relationship underscores the clinical relevance of gestational age in determining the likelihood of a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 109
retrieved_docs.content: disorders, and obstetric interventions together form a comprehensive framework for risk assessment, care planning, and management of maternal‑fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 683
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the network of relationships portrays diabetes as a multifaceted condition that influences and is influenced by genetic abnormalities, neonatal presentations, limb and ocular complications, metabolic comorbidities, diagnostic testing, and medication safety, all of which are essential considerations for comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 5 -- Community 2924
retrieved_docs.content: Databases are clinically associated with gestation twin, indicating that information systems and data repositories are linked to the clinical aspects and management of twin pregnancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of a liveborn infant encompasses a range of perinatal classifications that are linked through gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth status. A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a specific child category of liveborn, indicating that infants born alive at this stage are included within the broader liveborn group. The duration of gestation has an inverse‑isa relationship with liveborn, reflecting that shorter gestational periods are associated with distinct clinical classifications of liveborn infants. One major classification of liveborn infants is short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation. This category both classifies liveborn and is self‑referential, emphasizing its central role in describing infants born with reduced gestational length, low birth weight, or impaired growth. The disorder relating to short gestation AND/OR low birthweight is positioned as a parent of liveborn, indicating that this disorder underlies the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2865
retrieved_docs.content: Duration of gestation refers to the length of time a fetus remains in the uterus before birth, and the classification of a birth as liveborn is encompassed within this gestational timeframe; therefore, liveborn status is defined by the specific duration of gestation at delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 263
retrieved_docs.content: The medical concepts described are tightly linked through hierarchical and associative relationships that clarify how gestational timing, perinatal disorders, and reproductive health services intersect. The specific gestational interval “29‑30 weeks of gestation completed” is connected to the outcome category “liveborn,” indicating that a liveborn infant can result from pregnancies reaching this stage. Conversely, the broader classification “Disorder relating to short gestation AND/OR low birthweight” lists “liveborn” as its parent, positioning liveborn as a more general category that encompasses such disorders. “Duration of gestation” is defined as an inverse‑isa of “liveborn,” meaning that the liveborn status is a particular instance within the overall spectrum of gestational durations. Encounters with health services for reproductive reasons (ICD‑10 Z30‑Z39) also cite “liveborn” as a parent, reflecting that liveborn infants are a key focus of reproductive health care encounters. The

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2316
retrieved_docs.content: A poor reproductive history, reflecting previous obstetric complications or adverse outcomes, is clinically associated with an increased risk of preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). This connection highlights the importance of detailed reproductive assessments in prenatal care to identify women who may be more likely to experience early delivery and to apply targeted monitoring and preventive strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 189
retrieved_docs.content: each condition as a potential adverse event, enabling systematic risk assessment, targeted monitoring, operative decision‑making, and coordinated maternal‑fetal and newborn care aimed at reducing morbidity and improving outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1696
retrieved_docs.content: The condition ولادة قبل الأوان functions as a parent category for Female Reproductive System Disorder, indicating that disorders of the female reproductive system may arise in the context of premature birth. In addition, a CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN is clinically associated with ولادة قبل الأوان, suggesting that patients who experience premature birth may also present with an indeterminate type of caesarean section scar. Recognizing these interrelationships can help clinicians evaluate reproductive health and surgical history in individuals with premature birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 145
retrieved_docs.content: to the broader concept of fetal distress that influences maternal treatment (ضائقة جنينية تؤثر في علاج الأم), highlighting the bidirectional impact of fetal and maternal conditions. Maternal pregnancy complications as a whole are nested within a larger, unclassified group of maternal complications (مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم، غير مصنف في موضع آخر), emphasizing that many of these disorders are interrelated and may require coordinated assessment, risk stratification, and management across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and post‑partum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is categorized as a specific type of maternal condition that can complicate pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium, and it is considered a subcategory under the broader classification of other conditions or status of the mother complicating these reproductive processes.
